coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted efficient exfoliation of MXene and its composite for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Yu
Zhang, Meng
Yan, Hong
Zhang, Yu
Guo, Ruijie
Wang, Huifang
coredata.dc:description: MXene, as a promising electrode material, exhibits outstanding performance in supercapacitors because of its excellent chemical and physical properties. However, the conventional inefficient exfoliation methods and the blocked ion transmission channels caused by self-weight accumulation of MXene nanoflakes all severely limit its development. Here, this work reports an efficient microwave exfoliation method that requires only 90s to exfoliate multilayered MXene into few-layer MXene (2–3 nm) with large-size (4∼6 μm). Additionally, to enhance its capacitance and cycling stability, the exfoliated MXene was composited with graphene quantum dots, which shows a larger specific surface area, and importantly performs ultrahigh capacitance (343 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g) and excellent capacitance retention (100% after 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A/g). When using composite material as an anode, the assembled supercapacitor exhibits an excellent energy density of 35 Wh/kg at a power density of 384 W/kg with 107% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles. Not only does this work provide an efficient approach to exfoliate MXene, but it also prepares a highly promising material for energy storage materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted exfoliation
Few-layer MXene
Homogeneous composites
Ultra-high performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of composite materials by electrostatic self-assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of Ti3AlC2 etched by NaHF2 solution, (b, c) SEM images of layered Ti3C2Tx, (d) Schematic diagram of the microwave exfoliation principle and structure of G@MXene.

(a) XRD patterns of untreated and treated MXene under different conditions, (b) AFM image, and (c) thickness profile of MXene nanosheets (Freezed, 1000 W, 90 s), (d) SEM images of MXene nanosheets and EDS maps.

TEM image of GQDs (a), TEM (b), and HRTEM (c) images of composite material, XPS spectra of G@MXene (d–f) and MXene (g–i), (j) Schematic diagram of composite material morphology, (k) zeta potentials and (l) N2 adsorption-desorption curves of different samples.
(a) CV curves and (b) GCD curves for different electrodes, (c) capacitance varied with different ratio of GQDs and MXene, (d) capacitance retention of GQDs, MXene, and G-15@MXene.
(a) CV curves, (b) GCD curves, (c) EIS patterns, and (d) capacitance retention ratio and coulomb efficiency of G-15@MXene at 5 A/g.
(a) CV curves, (b) GCD curves, (c) specific capacity and coulombic efficiency at 2 A/g, and (d) Ragone plot for assembled supercapacitor.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted efficient exfoliation of MXene and its composite for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MXene, as a promising electrode material, exhibits outstanding performance in supercapacitors because of its excellent chemical and physical properties. However, the conventional inefficient exfoliation methods and the blocked ion transmission channels caused by self-weight accumulation of MXene nanoflakes all severely limit its development. Here, this work reports an efficient microwave exfoliation method that requires only 90s to exfoliate multilayered MXene into few-layer MXene (2–3 nm) with large-size (4∼6 μm). Additionally, to enhance its capacitance and cycling stability, the exfoliated MXene was composited with graphene quantum dots, which shows a larger specific surface area, and importantly performs ultrahigh capacitance (343 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g) and excellent capacitance retention (100% after 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A/g). When using composite material as an anode, the assembled supercapacitor exhibits an excellent energy density of 35 Wh/kg at a power density of 384 W/kg with 107% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles. Not only does this work provide an efficient approach to exfoliate MXene, but it also prepares a highly promising material for energy storage materials.

ce:section.ce:para: As the severe energy crisis continues to grow, many researchers are focusing on efficient energy storage materials. Supercapacitors, high-efficient, eco-friendly, safe, and long-life energy storage devices, have attracted widespread attention.

MXene is considered one of the most promising energy storage materials due to its unique microstructure and excellent performance [1–5]. Particularly, Ti3C2Tx, as the MXene typical representative material, has a bulk capacitance of 100 F/g, with excellent cycling stability and rate performance. However, its strong potential has never been fully realized due to the limitations of self-weight stacking of the layered structure, causing the insufficient ion-accessible surface for the electrolyte [6–9]. Notably, it is vital to exfoliate multilayered MXene into few-layer MXene. Ultrasonic exfoliation is not only time-consuming but also extremely difficult to control. Few-layer MXene exfoliated by traditional methods is usually thicker and has smaller lateral dimensions. Additionally, increasing the specific surface area by intercalation and thus providing more space for ion transport and storage also provides a solution to this problem, many materials, such as conductive polymers, carbon materials, aerogels, and double-layered hydroxides, etc., were used to improve the energy storage properties of MXene [10–13]. For instance, Boota et al. inserted polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (PPy), and Poly(3,4- ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) into MXene layers to achieve capacitance values of 57, 59, and 47 F/g at a scan rate of 5 mV/s, respectively [14]. Le et al. used PPy nanowires wrapping MXene to prepare a high-quality capacitive composite of 610 F/g [15]. Subsequently, Yu et al. intercalated activated carbon particles between MXene layers and prepared a composite with a capacitance value of 126 F/g and a power density of 17.8 Wh/kg [16]. Although the different research teams have worked tirelessly to achieve the desired performance, it is still a great challenge to obtain large-size few-layer MXene and a composite with a high specific surface area in order to expect outstanding energy storage properties.

The microwave method is an efficient and environment-friendly method that has been used widely in the preparation of material in recent years [17,18]. This work provides a new way, using the energy of microwaves to rapidly evaporate interlayer water, to obtain a larger lateral size few-layer MXene in a very short time (just 90 s) than that obtained by conventional exfoliation methods. Interlayer water evaporation generated a driving force greater than the force between the layers to exfoliate multilayered MXene. Meanwhile, the principle of binary synergy and complementarity was applied to prepare a composite material that satisfies both high capacitance values and capacitance retention, taking advantage of MXene layered structure for ion transport and GQD high capacitance (595 F/g) [19–23]. More importantly, composites with large specific surface areas enhanced the contact area between the active substance and the electrolyte during electrochemical work in order to provide more interfaces for ion adsorption and desorption. Additionally, the introduction of quantum dots not only served to provide high capacitance values and stopped the self-weight build-up of MXene, but was also evenly distributed on the surface of MXene, with some of the quantum dots covering the bare Ti atoms on the MXene surface, preventing structural damage caused by its oxidation. G-15@MXene (with 15 wt% GQDs) electrode made by this work exhibits ultrahigh capacitance of 343 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g and 100% capacitance retention at a current density of 5 A/g over 10,000 cycles. A supercapacitor assembled with G-15@MXene and activated carbon shows an ultrahigh energy density of 35 Wh/kg at a power density of 384 W/kg, which is greater than the highest values reported for MXene-based materials. This work provides a new method for high-efficient exfoliation to get large few-layer MXene nanosheets, as well as a promising electrode material with high capacitance and rate performance for rapid energy storage.

After comparing several etching methods [24,25],Ti3C2Tx was prepared from Ti3AlC2 using a method proposed by our group [26]. 300 μL of deionized water was added into 0.3 g of Ti3C2Tx powder (Figure S1). The mixture was well mixed using a vortex mixer, followed by being frozen in a refrigerator. Then, a crucible with the frozen powder surrounded by CuO powder was placed inside a large crucible, which was put inside a microwave oven. Few-layer Ti3C2Tx powder was prepared by microwave-assisted exfoliation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of composite materials by electrostatic self-assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The solution for electrostatic self-assembly was obtained by adding 0.1147 g of NaCl (Damao Chemical Company), 1.4320 g of poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride (PDDA Mw100000∼200000, 20 wt%, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.), and 0.2407 g of C4H11NO3 (Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) into a solution of 85 mL deionized water and 15 mL absolute ethanol [27,28]. The above solution was sonicated for 3 h, and then a certain amount (10, 15, 20, 25 wt% of exfoliated Ti3C2Tx) of GQDs (kindly provided by Liaoning Hengtong Graphene Technology Co., Ltd) was added. The amount of reagent used to modify the GQDs is tiny and therefore has no effect on the product. The mixture was mechanically stirred for 24 h. Next, the exfoliated Ti3C2Tx powder was added to the mixture, which was stirred for another 24 h. The reaction was washed several times with DI water by filtration in order to remove the free quantum dots. The final products were filtered and dried to obtain the G@MXene composites.

The composite, acetylene black, and poly(1,1-difluoroethylene) (PVDF) were mixed in a ratio of 8:1:1. The mixed slurry was coated onto nickel foam (20 × 20 × 1 mm). The G@MXene electrodes were dried in a drying oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The amount of active material on the working electrode was roughly 4 mg/cm2, which is very close to the areal mass loading for the commercial application [29]. This value was obtained by subtracting the mass of the original nickel foam from the mass of the electrode sheet after drying. Similarly, the nickel foam coated with activated carbon (carbon electrode) was prepared.

The electrochemical testing of electrode material was done in a three-electrode system consisting of a reference electrode (Hg/HgO electrode), a counter electrode (platinum sheet), and a working electrode (the G@MXene electrode). In addition, the performance of the supercapacitors was tested in a two-electrode system with the G@MXene electrode as an anode and the carbon electrode as a cathode. 3 M KOH solution was applied as electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured at an AC disturbance amplitude of 5 mV in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz [30].

The gravimetric capacitance was calculated from the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curve. Equation (1) is as follows.
(1)
C g
=
4 I Δ t
m Δ V
where I is the value of current, Δt is the discharge point time, m is the mass load per unit area, and ΔV is the potential window.

The energy density and the power density are two other important indicators for evaluating the properties of supercapacitors. The calculation is shown in Equations (2) and (3), respectively.
(2)
E
( W h · k
g
− 1
)
=
C g
( Δ V )
2
2 × 3.6
(3)
P
( W · k
g
− 1
)
=
3600 × E
t
where E is the energy density, P is the power density and t is the total charge and discharge time [31].

Fig. 1 (a) shows the schematic diagram of Ti3AlC2 etched by NaHF2 solution and the layered Ti3C2Tx is observed in SEM images (Fig. 1(b) and c). The exfoliation of Ti3C2Tx in this work was achieved by the energy of the microwave [32]. Fig. 1(d) shows the mechanism of exfoliation and the morphology of the composite. It is well documented that interlayer water was still present in MXene after drying [33]. Multilayered MXene placed in a microwave field, interlayer water absorbs intense energy in the small interlayer space. The vaporization of interlayered water produced robust instantaneous expansion work, which was stronger than the bonding force of the H-bond between the layers, and the multilayered MXene was consequently exfoliated into few-layer MXene. The control of time and power of the microwave avoided damage to the lateral dimensions. It can be observed that the volume of few-layer MXene is significantly bigger than that of the same mass of multilayered MXene (Figure S2).

The XRD patterns show the difference of the microwave-assisted exfoliation affected by treatment time, with or without freezing. Fig. 2 (a) shows that the peak corresponding to the (002) lattice plane shifted from 9.58° (MAX phase) to 7.04° (MXene) [26]. Along with the exfoliation process, 002 peak has a shift to the left and then disappears. This phenomenon can be attributed to the rapid exfoliation of multilayered MXene into few-layer MXene and the destruction of the original multilayer structure leading to the disappearance of a peak in the range of 5–10°. XRD patterns for other exfoliating conditions can be seen in Figure S3. In Fig. 2(b), two MXene nanosheets were stacked, with lateral size of 5 μm and a thickness of 2–3 nm for each. Fig. 2(d) shows many few-layer nanosheets in a large size within the field of view. Noteworthily, direct treatment of dry powder under microwave severely damaged the lateral dimensions of MXene (Figure S4). So after several attempts, it was found that adding a proper amount of water to MXene powder and freezing it before microwave treatment ensured large lateral dimensions of nanosheets. Water between the layers gained microwave energy to instantly evaporate. The layers were peeled off from each other by the instantaneous expansion work from evaporation of interlayered water. A little water added on the powder surface was to absorb excess energy to prevent damage to the lateral dimensions. Fig. 2(e–f) shows three sequentially enlarged images. The EDS maps indicate the uniform distribution of all elements. The sizes of the nanosheets observed from the SEM images were in accordance with those from the AFM image (Fig. 2(g)). The few-layer MXene can be clearly seen in the TEM images (Fig. 2(h)∼j, Figure S5). The Fourier transform (FFT) pattern shows the hexagonal symmetry of Ti3C2Tx. The lattice spacing (d = 0.272 ± 0.03 nm) can be seen in Fig. 2(j), corresponding to the few-layer MXene. These characterizations are direct evidence of microwave high-efficient exfoliation to obtain few-layer MXene.

The TEM images provide direct evidence of the formation of the composite. Fig. 3 (a) shows spherical GQDs in the agglomerated state in a diameter of approximately 8 nm [34]. Fig. 3(b) illustrates that GQDs were uniformly covered on the surface of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. The lattice spacing (d = 0.204 ± 0.04 nm) can be seen in Fig. 3(c), corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of the graphene quantum dots [35,36]. TEM images further demonstrate the uniform distribution of GQDs on MXene nanosheets. In addition, SEM images of composite materials can be found in Figure S6. Compared to MXene, the composite G@MXene had a significant increase in the content of elemental C and O (Fig.d∼i). The C 1s core-level peak could be fitted into seven peaks at 280.5, 281.4, 283.5, 284.8, 286.1, 287.9, and 289.5 eV, respectively assigned to C–Ti, Ti–C–O, CC, C–C, C–O, CO and C–F bonds [26,30]. In the composite, the C–O peak appeared due to the partial oxidation during the microwave treatment. In addition, the introduction of GQDs resulted in a significant increase in CC content. Ti 2p core-level spectrum presents six fitted peaks. The peaks located at 454.4, 455.5, and 458.4 eV corresponded to Ti–C, Ti–N, and TiO2 bonds, respectively [30]. The formation of Ti–N was inevitable since the etching process was done in a nitrogen atmosphere [26]. The TiO2 content of the composites was significantly reduced, while the ratio of Ti–C bonds was slightly increased. Due to the addition of graphene quantum dots, it was covered the metal atoms that would otherwise be exposed on the surface of MXene, avoiding the structural damage and degradation of the properties caused by the oxidation of the Ti atoms. Compared to the insertion of graphene sheets, this work could achieve more structurally stable composite material. Similarly, the increase in Ti–C bonding confirmed the above statement. The composites were prepared by electrostatic self-assembly of modified GQDs and few-layer MXene nanosheets [27,28].

Fig. 3 shows (k) Zeta potentials and (l) N2 adsorption and desorption curves of different samples. MXene nanosheets were negatively charged (−14 mV), PDDA-modified GQDs were positively charged (+41 mV), and the Zeta potential of composite G-15@MXene was +29 mV, suggesting a successful synthesis of composite. The N2 adsorption isotherm showed a distinct type IV curve [29,31]. Obviously, the specific surface area of the composite (25.18 m2/g) was much greater than those of MXene (13.70 m2/g) and exfoliated MXene (18.17 m2/g). Also, this value was greater than that of the reported Ti3C2Tx/PPy [37]. GQDs effectively prevented Ti3C2Tx nanosheets from self-weight stacking. Such a structure can provide more active sites and a highly interconnected path for electrolyte ions to swiftly transport, which will be immensely promising for fast energy storage applications [19,38]. Several characterization methods together demonstrate the formation of composite materials.
Theoretically, the structure of G@MXene could largely improve the mass capacitance, rate performance, and energy density. The following gives the electrochemical performance of the composite.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the CV curves for the six electrodes at a scan rate of 100 mV/s from −0.1 to 0.4 V. The area enclosed by the CV curve of GQDs was the largest, and the second one was enclosed by the curve of G-15@MXene. The GCD curves at 1 A/g are shown in Fig. 4(b). The absence of a significant voltage drop at the start of charging and discharging was proof that they were double-layer capacitors [6,39]. Capacitance values were calculated for different samples based on discharge curves of 1 A/g. The extremely high specific surface of GQDs results in its highest capacitance value (599 F/g) [20–23]. The MXene electrode only had a capacitance of 134 F/g. However, the capacitance values for different composites were 343 F/g (G-15@MXene), 310 F/g (G-25@MXene), 301 F/g (G-20@MXene), and 265 F/g (G-10@MXene), respectively, among which G-15@MXene had the best capacitance. It was reported that a capacitance would be declined at a higher mass loading [29]. Most of the reported mass loading in literature was 1 mg/cm2. Interestingly, the G-15@MXene composite retained an ultrahigh capacitance (343 F/g) at the mass loading of 4 mg/cm2, which was superior to many reported electrode materials.

As shown in Fig. 4(c), the six electrodes exhibited a similar trend of decreased capacitance with an increased current density. Likewise, the G-15@MXene had the highest specific capacity at different current densities. At a lower loading, the surfaces of MXene nanosheets were not fully covered by GQDs. At a higher loading, however, more GQDs aggregated together to partially block passages between layers, leading to a decrease in capacitance. The cycling stability of GQDs, MXene, and G-15@MXene electrodes was examined at a current density of 5 A/g for 10,000 cycles. MXene performed much better capacitance retention than GQDs at 10,000 cycles due to its layered structure (Fig. 4(d)). The MXene used in this work was etched with NaHF2, and the presence of Na+ ions between the layers caused the layer spacing to expand further. In addition, the insertion/de-intercalation of ions during the charge/discharge cycle resulted in an increase in the layer space, facilitating the diffusion of ions. These reasons provided a significant improvement in MXene capacitance retention. However, its disadvantage was its low capacitance. In contrast, GQDs had a high capacitance but low capacitance retention, with a 70% retention ratio over 6000 cycles. As expected, the G-15@MXene exhibited a high capacitance and 100% retention ratio over 10000 cycles. It follows that the introduction of GQDs into the few-layer MXene not only enhanced the capacitance value of MXene but also overcame the defect of low capacitance retention of GQDs. Next, G-15@MXene was chosen in this work to test the electrochemical properties. The rectangular-like and symmetrical CV curves measured at scan rates of 5–100 mV/s (Fig. 5 (a)) demonstrated its excellent capacitive performance. The capacitance values of G-15@MXene calculated from GCD curves (Fig. 5(b)) were 343 F/g at 1 A/g, 146 F/g at 2 A/g, 119 F/g at 5 A/g, and 100 F/g at 10 A/g, respectively. Fig. 5(c) shows EIS patterns of GQDs, MXene, and G-15@MXene with an inset of an enlargement of the high-frequency region. The curves in the high-frequency region represent the charge transfer resistance of the substance, while the sloping lines in the low-frequency region represent the diffusion resistance. MXene showed the highest charge transfer and diffusion resistance. The introduction of GQDs greatly enhanced the conductivity of MXene by reducing the charge transfer and diffusion resistance.

Compared with MXene, the G-15@MXene was favorable to rapid ion transfer and diffusion, contributing to much higher capacitance and better rate properties. Fig. 5(d) shows the capacitance retention and Coulomb efficiency of G-15@MXene over 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A/g. The inset shows the CV curves of G-15@MXene at the 50th and the 10,000th cycles. The areas enclosed by these two curves were almost the same, confirming 100% of the capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles. Moreover, the Coulomb efficiency remained stable at 100% throughout the cycling process. In other words, the G-15@MXene had a fast ion transfer capability over a long period of multiple charge/discharge without self-weight stacking of MXene nanosheets, thanks to the uniform distribution of GQDs.

Subsequently, the performance of the assembled supercapacitor with G-15@MXene was tested in two stages (Fig. 6 (a)). The CV curves of the assembled supercapacitors were first tested within a voltage window of −0.1 to 0.4 V, still maintaining a rectangular-like shape. The voltage window was further increased to 1.5 V to get close to practical application. A pair of redox peaks occurred within the increased voltage window, indicating the reversible insertion and removal of ions and the change in the valence. Also, as the increasing of scan rate, the redox peaks shifted a little, still demonstrating excellent rate performance. The almost perfectly symmetrical charge and discharge curves in Fig. 6(b) show that the electrode materials remained good reversibility in the assembled supercapacitor. The assembled supercapacitor had a specific capacitance of 112 F/g at 1 A/g, 101 F/g at 2 A/g, 86 F/g at 5 A/g, and 69 F/g at 10 A/g. At a current density of 2 A/g, the specific capacity and coulombic efficiency after 10,000 cycles are shown in Fig. 6(c). The increase in specific capacity after 8000 cycles may be due to the increase of layer spacing of the composite caused by the ion insertion/removal process. Importantly, the assembled supercapacitor maintained a 107% of capacitance retention rate over 10,000 cycles, indicating that the assembled supercapacitor had high cycling stability at a high capacitance value. In addition, the power density and the energy density are also important indicators to evaluate the performance of supercapacitors. The Ragone plot in Fig. 6(d) shows the energy density of the supercapacitor in this work at different power densities. The asymmetric supercapacitors achieved a maximum energy density of 35.00 Wh/kg at a power density of 384 W/kg. The energy density got down with an increased power density. However, a relatively high energy density of 21.56 Wh/kg was obtained at 3815 W/kg. Some MXene-based supercapacitors in the literature were selected for comparison [14,16,29,30,40]. Because of a larger voltage window resulting in a higher energy density; it can be seen that the performance of the assembled supercapacitor in this work is much higher than the highest values reported for MXene-based supercapacitors.

In summary，we designed a microwave-assisted exfoliation method, using interlayer water vaporization to exfoliate MXene under the microwave，which can efficiently obtain large-scale few-layer nanosheets. Meanwhile, MXene nanosheets and graphene quantum dots are electrostatically self-assembled to form lamellar structures with stable electronic channels. The resultant G-15@MXene exhibits an excellent capacitance of 343 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The assembled supercapacitor performs excellent capacitance of 112 F/g at 1 A/g. Both perform 100% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles. More importantly, the supercapacitor in this work achieves an energy density of 35 Wh/kg at a power density of 384 W/kg, superior to those previously reported MXene-based supercapacitors. The highly efficient exfoliation of MXene by microwave is promising for large-scale industrial production, and the GQDs@MXene hybrid has great potential for rapid energy storage devices with high capacitance and long-term cycling stability.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2500) was performed with Cu Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) collected on a powder diffractometer, with a scanning speed of 4°/min in the range 2θ = 4–80°. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPA-300HV, Seiko, Japan) was used to observe the shape and thickness of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with monolayer or few layers. The morphology of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets was observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Jeol JSM-7100F). The nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90) was used to measure the potential of different samples. The specific surface area of different samples was determined using the Mac ASAP 2460. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, Al Kα-ray) was used to analyze chemical bonds in the samples. The Ti3C2Tx flakes were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos-S) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Co.) was used for testing the performance of electrode materials and assembled supercapacitors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 1703152 and 22178242), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201701D121030). Liaoning Hengtong Graphene Technology Co., Ltd is thanked for kindly providing graphene quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2021.12.149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clay-inspired MXene-based electrochemical devices and photo-electrocatalyst: state-of-the-art progresses and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, Y. Wu, X. Yuan, G. Zeng, J. Zhou, X. Wang, J.W. Chew, Clay-Inspired MXene-Based Electrochemical Devices and Photo-Electrocatalyst: State-of-the-Art Progresses and Challenges, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) e1704561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoengineering of 2D MXene-based materials for energy storage applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Nan, X. Guo, J. Xiao, X. Li, W. Chen, W. Wu, H. Liu, Y. Wang, M. Wu, G. Wang, Nanoengineering of 2D MXene-Based Materials for Energy Storage Applications, Small 17 (2021) e1902085.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of two-dimensional transition metal carbide/nitride (MXene) hybrids and nanocomposites for catalytic energy storage and conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.R.G. Lim, A.D. Handoko, S.K. Nemani, B. Wyatt, H.Y. Jiang, J. Tang, B. Anasori, Z.W. Seh, Rational Design of Two-Dimensional Transition Metal Carbide/Nitride (MXene) Hybrids and Nanocomposites for Catalytic Energy Storage and Conversion, ACS Nano 14 (2020) 10834-10864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of MXenes as new nanomaterials for energy storage/delivery and selected environmental applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assembling 2D MXenes into highly stable pseudocapacitive electrodes with high power and energy densities
